      Is Wall Street boring?
   =20
 =20
In =E2=80=9CLiar=E2=80=99s Poker,=E2=80=9D Michael Lewis memorably describe=
s showing up at work for his first day as a bond salesman at Salomon Brothe=
rs. =E2=80=9COddly enough, I didn=E2=80=99t really imagine I was going to w=
ork, more as if I were going to collect lottery winnings=E2=80=9D:A chaired=
 professor of the London School of Economics, who took a keen interest in m=
aterial affairs, stared at me bug-eyed and gurgled when he heard what I was=
 to be paid. It was twice what he earned. He was in his mid-forties and at =
the top of his profession. I was twenty-four years old and at the bottom of=
 mine. There was no justice in the world, and thank goodness for that.Perha=
ps it is worth explaining where this money was coming from, not that I gave=
 it much thought at the time. Man for man Salomon Brothers was, in 1985, th=
e world=E2=80=99s most profitable corporation. At least that is what I was =
repeatedly told. I never bothered to check it because it seemed so obviousl=
y true. Wall Street was hot. And we were Wall Street=E2=80=99s most profita=
ble firm.At Bloomberg today,  Max Abelson writes about the fact that Wall S=
treet is in some objective sense hot, but nobody feels like they=E2=80=99ve=
 won the lottery:The biggest U.S. banks are putting finishing touches on th=
eir most profitable year ever and preparing to dole out some massive bonuse=
s, yet the usual sounds of Wall Street=E2=80=99s backslapping and toasts ha=
ve faded. They=E2=80=99re not only hushed by exhaustion from nearly two yea=
rs of global pandemic, or even wariness of conspicuous consumption in an ag=
e of vast inequality. Industry denizens describe a sense that assignments a=
re unending, trouble is brewing and the real fun is elsewhere.Veterans of p=
ast Wall Street booms suggest that, somehow, this one doesn=E2=80=99t feel =
so good. One reason: The wealth reeled in by elite traders and dealmakers i=
s getting outshined by the quick riches touted by cryptocurrency fanatics, =
fintech whizzes and meme stocks. ...=E2=80=9CThere=E2=80=99s always somebod=
y doing better than you,=E2=80=9D said Mark Gorton, the founder and chairma=
n of high-frequency trading firm Tower Research Capital. The number of teen=
agers worth $20 million because they giddily loaded up on crypto can be har=
d on the ego. =E2=80=9CEveryone compares themselves to those people,=E2=80=
=9D Gorton added. =E2=80=9CI kind of consciously try not to.=E2=80=9DThat e=
nvy marks a shift from only about a year ago, when Sam Peurifoy sensed coll=
eagues at Goldman were treating crypto as =E2=80=9Ckind of a cute, niche cu=
riosity.=E2=80=9D Known for his gaming persona Das Kapitalist, Peurifoy lef=
t around June for Floating Point Group, which provides services for trading=
 digital currency, and is now an executive at Hivemind, a $1.5 billion cryp=
to fund. Despite bankers=E2=80=99 windfall year, Peurifoy said there=E2=80=
=99s a =E2=80=9Cfeeling in the air that they are missing out,=E2=80=9D desc=
ribing it not as a bleakness but =E2=80=9Cthis overwhelming =E2=80=98Wow.=
=E2=80=99=E2=80=9DI think that the basic appeal of Wall Street for a long t=
ime =E2=80=94=C2=A0certainly in the mid-1980s, when Lewis started, but also=
 in the mid-2000s, when I started =E2=80=94=C2=A0was not just that it paid =
well, but specifically that it was a great machine for turning general clev=
erness into money. There have always been people who got rich because they =
had a great idea for a product that people wanted and were able to turn it =
into reality, but the financial industry spent decades making quite a lot o=
f people rich because they were pretty good at solving puzzles. It was hard=
 work, but you could get richer faster than, you know, a doctor, and withou=
t the singleness of vision of an entrepreneur. It could feel like not quite=
 earning an honest living; it could feel like getting rich for playing a ga=
me well.Now that thing is so obviously crypto, and so obviously not mergers=
-and-acquisitions banking or whatever, that being an M&A banker just feels =
like a regular job. Who wants that? The lottery winners are all over in cry=
pto, ostentatiously winning the lottery every day.

One thing that I  like to  say around here is that crypto rapidly recapitul=
ates the history, and re-learns the lessons, of traditional finance. I don=
=E2=80=99t particularly mean this as a bad thing. Learning is good! Speedru=
nning all of financial history from scratch allows you to make different, i=
nteresting, sometimes better choices about what to do about those lessons. =
Also most of those lessons were learned a long time ago and are now sort of=
 buried tacit knowledge; the traditional financial system does lots of thin=
gs, and it does most of them for good reasons, but often most people have f=
orgotten what those reasons are. Crypto provides frequent convenient entert=
aining reminders.Anyway Turkey is doing  a weird currency thing:President R=
ecep Tayyip Erdogan=E2=80=99s government announced extraordinary measures t=
o bolster the Turkish lira, including the introduction of a new program tha=
t will protect savings from fluctuations in the local currency.The governme=
nt will make up for losses incurred by holders of lira deposits should the =
lira=E2=80=99s declines against hard currencies exceed interest rates promi=
sed by banks, Erdogan said after chairing a cabinet meeting in Ankara. ...=
=E2=80=9CFrom now on, none of our citizens will need to switch their deposi=
ts from the Turkish lira to foreign currencies because of their concerns th=
at the exchange rate=E2=80=9D fluctuations might wipe out gains from intere=
st payments, Erdogan said.The measures are intended to mitigate retail inve=
stors=E2=80=99 demand for dollars and bring to an end three months of turmo=
il for the nation=E2=80=99s currency.=C2=A0Bloomberg explains:The Treasury =
will make up for losses incurred by holders of lira deposits should the lir=
a=E2=80=99s declines against hard currencies exceed bank interest rates. Fo=
r example, if banks pay 15% for one-year lira deposits but the currency dep=
reciates 20% against the dollar in the same period, the Treasury -- that is=
, taxpayers -- would pay deposit-holders the differential. The instrument w=
ill apply for individuals holding lira deposit accounts with maturities bet=
ween three to 12 months. The minimum interest rate will be the central bank=
=E2=80=99s benchmark rate and no withholding tax will be implemented.To get=

Man, spoofing is such a weird crime. The idea of spoofing is that a thing i=

id to buy 100 at $99, and you also put in an offer to sell 1,000 of them at=

=9Cwow, there is an imbalance of supply and demand, more sellers than buyer=
s, the price will probably go down,=E2=80=9D so they will lower their price=
=C2=A0and start selling at $99. So you=E2=80=99ll be able to buy 100 things=
 at $99. And then you cancel your order to sell 1,000 things at $101 and go=

pulation. It is a crime because you are=C2=A0misrepresenting your intention=
s. You say =E2=80=94=C2=A0implicitly, by putting in the order =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0that you want to sell 1,000 things at $101, but you don=E2=80=99t. That =
is not your real desire; your real desire is to buy 100 things at $99. The =
sell order is a spoof, a fake-out, a lie designed to get people to trade wi=
th your real buy order at lower prices than they otherwise would.An objecti=
on to this theory is that your 1,000-thing sell order is absolutely=C2=A0re=
al, in the sense that it is a live order on a live market. If you put in an=
 order to sell 1,000 things at $101, and some buyer is like =E2=80=9Cdone, =
I=E2=80=99ll take them,=E2=80=9D then guess what, you sold 1,000 things. Yo=

putting in orders that do not reflect your real desires. But in another sen=
se spoof orders create=C2=A0real=C2=A0supply and demand, since you are putt=
ing in orders that people can trade with.Anyway yesterday NatWest Markets P=

n. Here is how prosecutors describe the crime:By placing Spoof Orders, the =

ing information was intended to, and at times did, trick other market parti=

mand by buying and selling U.S. Treasuries at quantities, prices, and times=

e NatWest traders got caught is that they discussed spoofing in their onlin=

fed =E2=80=99em=E2=80=9D is strong, but it is not as strong as the temptati=
on to=C2=A0complain=C2=A0in the chat when you mess up the crime:Trader-1 so=
metimes referenced his deceptive trading practices in electronic chats with=

illed by other market participants despite his intentions and before he cou=
ld cancel them. For instance, in a chat on June 13, 2011, he explained to a=

 a buy order (a =E2=80=9Cbid=E2=80=9D) into the market. The colleague asked=
, =E2=80=9Cwhy you try and bid? to spoof?=E2=80=9D Trader-1 answered: =E2=
=80=9Cy[es] . . . i was doing lot of that last week & was saying myself, go=
nna get caught soon, should stop.=E2=80=9D In a chat two weeks later, on Ju=
ne 29, 2011, Trader1 mentioned to the same colleague that he had been =E2=
=80=9Ccauight spoofing few times,=E2=80=9D and in a chat on August 15, 2012=

 account by exercising options) and 173.5 million shares of =E2=80=9Cindire=
ct=E2=80=9D ownership (shares he owns in his revocable trust), plus=C2=A0ye=
sterday=E2=80=99s Form 4 showing 2.1 million shares of option exercise and =
about 0.6 million of sales.[3] In some economic sense he already owned=C2=
=A0the shares underlying those options: The options were freely exercisable=
 and enormously in-the-money, so he as good as owned the shares. But from a=
 Form 4 actual share ownership perspective, he did not own the shares and n=
ow he does.[4] =E2=80=9CIs anything that the chief executive officer of a p=
ublic company says on Twitter legally binding, because if it is not true th=
en that=E2=80=99s securities fraud,=E2=80=9D  I asked in my discussion ques=
tions about his poll, and I think the answer might be yes?

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Is Wall Street boring?</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In =E2=80=9CLiar=E2=80=99s Poker,=E2=
=80=9D Michael Lewis <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cli=
ck/26137490.286658/aHR0cHM6Ly9ib29rcy5nb29nbGUuY29tL2Jvb2tzP2lkPUxwUzlBd0FB=
UUJBSiZwZz1QVDQzJmxwZz1QVDQzI3Y9b25lcGFnZSZxJmY9ZmFsc2U/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cB4d2c1f76" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;">memorably describes</a> showing up at work for his =
first day as a bond salesman at Salomon Brothers. =E2=80=9COddly enough, I =
didn=E2=80=99t really imagine I was going to work, more as if I were going =
to collect lottery winnings=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">A chaired professor of the London School of Economics, who took a ke=
en interest in material affairs, stared at me bug-eyed and gurgled when he =
heard what I was to be paid. It was twice what he earned. He was in his mid=
-forties and at the top of his profession. I was twenty-four years old and =
at the bottom of mine. There was no justice in the world, and thank goodnes=
s for that.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Perhaps it is worth explaining =
where this money was coming from, not that I gave it much thought at the ti=
me. Man for man Salomon Brothers was, in 1985, the world=E2=80=99s most pro=
fitable corporation. At least that is what I was repeatedly told. I never b=
othered to check it because it seemed so obviously true. Wall Street was ho=
t. And we were Wall Street=E2=80=99s most profitable firm.</p></blockquote>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At Bloomberg today, <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26137490.286658/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJn=
LmNvbS9uZXdzL2ZlYXR1cmVzLzIwMjEtMTItMjIvMjAyMS1icm91Z2h0LXdhbGwtc3RyZWV0LXJ=
lY29yZC1wcm9maXRzLWJpZ2dlci1ib251c2VzLWFuZC1hLWxvdC1vZi1kcmVhZD9jbXBpZD1CQk=
QxMjIyMjFfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlc=
iZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMTEyMjImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cBe011ff5d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> Max Abelson writes</a> about the fact that Wall Str=
eet is in some objective sense hot, but nobody feels like they=E2=80=99ve w=
on the lottery:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The biggest U.S=
. banks are putting finishing touches on their most profitable year ever an=
d preparing to dole out some massive bonuses, yet the usual sounds of Wall =
Street=E2=80=99s backslapping and toasts have faded. They=E2=80=99re not on=
ly hushed by exhaustion from nearly two years of global pandemic, or even w=
ariness of conspicuous consumption in an age of vast inequality. Industry d=
enizens describe a sense that assignments are unending, trouble is brewing =
and the real fun is elsewhere.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Veterans of =
past Wall Street booms suggest that, somehow, this one doesn=E2=80=99t feel=
 so good. One reason: The wealth reeled in by elite traders and dealmakers =
is getting outshined by the quick riches touted by cryptocurrency fanatics,=
 fintech whizzes and meme stocks. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=
=80=9CThere=E2=80=99s always somebody doing better than you,=E2=80=9D said =
Mark Gorton, the founder and chairman of high-frequency trading firm Tower =
Research Capital. The number of teenagers worth $20 million because they gi=
ddily loaded up on crypto can be hard on the ego. =E2=80=9CEveryone compare=
s themselves to those people,=E2=80=9D Gorton added. =E2=80=9CI kind of con=
sciously try not to.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That envy mar=
ks a shift from only about a year ago, when Sam Peurifoy sensed colleagues =
at Goldman were treating crypto as =E2=80=9Ckind of a cute, niche curiosity=
.=E2=80=9D Known for his gaming persona Das Kapitalist, Peurifoy left aroun=
d June for Floating Point Group, which provides services for trading digita=
l currency, and is now an executive at Hivemind, a $1.5 billion crypto fund=
. Despite bankers=E2=80=99 windfall year, Peurifoy said there=E2=80=99s a =
=E2=80=9Cfeeling in the air that they are missing out,=E2=80=9D describing =
it not as a bleakness but =E2=80=9Cthis overwhelming =E2=80=98Wow.=E2=80=99=
=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think that the bas=
ic appeal of Wall Street for a long time =E2=80=94=C2=A0certainly in the mi=
d-1980s, when Lewis started, but also in the mid-2000s, when I started =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0was not just that it paid well, but specifically that it was a =
great machine for turning general cleverness into money. There have always =
been people who got rich because they had a great idea for a product that p=
eople wanted and were able to turn it into reality, but the financial indus=
try spent decades making quite a lot of people rich because they were prett=
y good at solving puzzles. It was hard work, but you could get richer faste=
r than, you know, a doctor, and without the singleness of vision of an entr=
epreneur. It could feel like not quite earning an honest living; it could f=
eel like getting rich for playing a game well.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">Now <em>that</em> thing is so obviously crypto, and so obviously not me=
rgers-and-acquisitions banking or whatever, that being an M&A banker ju=
st feels like a regular job. Who wants that? The lottery winners are all ov=
er in crypto, ostentatiously winning the lottery every day.</p><table class=

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Man, spoofing is such a weird crime. T=
he idea of spoofing is that a thing is trading at $100, and you want to buy=
 100 of them cheap, so you put in a bid to buy 100 at $99, and you also put=
 in an offer to sell 1,000 of them at $101. The theory is that people will =
see those two orders and think =E2=80=9Cwow, there is an imbalance of suppl=
y and demand, more sellers than buyers, the price will probably go down,=E2=
=80=9D so they will lower their price=C2=A0and start selling at $99. So you=
=E2=80=99ll be able to buy 100 things at $99. And then you cancel your orde=
r to sell 1,000 things at $101 and go on your merry way.</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">This is, the theory goes, a crime, a form of market manipulat=
ion. It is a crime because you are=C2=A0<em>misrepresenting your intentions=
</em>. You say =E2=80=94=C2=A0implicitly, by putting in the order =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0that you want to sell 1,000 things at $101, but you don=E2=80=99t. Th=
at is not your real desire; your real desire is to buy 100 things at $99. T=
he sell order is a spoof, a fake-out, a lie designed to get people to trade=
 with your real buy order at lower prices than they otherwise would.</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An objection to this theory is that your 1,000-th=
ing sell order is absolutely=C2=A0<em>real</em>, in the sense that it is a =
live order on a live market. If you put in an order to sell 1,000 things at=
 $101, and some buyer is like =E2=80=9Cdone, I=E2=80=99ll take them,=E2=80=
=9D then guess what, you sold 1,000 things. You didn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>wan=
t=C2=A0</em>to =E2=80=94=C2=A0your subjective intention was to buy =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0but you put in the order and you got lifted.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">In a sense spoof orders create an illusion of supply and demand,=
 since you are putting in orders that do not reflect your real desires. But=
 in another sense spoof orders create=C2=A0<em>real=C2=A0</em>supply and de=
mand, since you are putting in orders that <em>people can trade with.</em><=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway yesterday NatWest Markets Plc <a hre=
f=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26137490.286658/aHR0cHM6=

yYXVkLXVzLXRyZWFzdXJ5LW1hcmtldHM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0cd2d965" itempro=
p=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
>pleaded guilty</a> to U.S. criminal spoofing charges for doing some spoofi=
ng in the Treasury futures and spot markets, agreeing to pay about $35 mill=
ion. Here is how prosecutors <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/26137490.286658/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdGljZS5nb3Yvb3BhL3ByZXNzLXJl=
bGVhc2UvZmlsZS8xNDU3OTgxL2Rvd25sb2Fk/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd22cad4d" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">describe the crime</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By=
 placing Spoof Orders, the Subject NatWest Traders intended to inject mater=
ially false and misleading information about the genuine supply and demand =
for U.S. Treasuries into the markets, and to deceive other participants in =
those markets into believing something untrue, namely that the visible orde=
r book accurately reflected market-based forces of supply and demand.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This materially false and misleading information=
 was intended to, and at times did, trick other market participants into re=
acting to the apparent change and imbalance in supply and demand by buying =
and selling U.S. Treasuries at quantities, prices, and times that they othe=
rwise likely would not have traded.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">But one way that the NatWest traders got caught is that they di=
scussed spoofing in their online chats when they got filled on the wrong si=
de. For traders, the temptation to do a crime and then get on the chat and =
say =E2=80=9Csweet, I just spoofed =E2=80=99em=E2=80=9D is strong, but it i=
s not as strong as the temptation to=C2=A0<em>complain=C2=A0</em>in the cha=
t when you mess up the crime:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">T=
rader-1 sometimes referenced his deceptive trading practices in electronic =
chats with colleagues at NATWEST MARKETS PLC, especially when his Spoof Ord=
ers were filled by other market participants despite his intentions and bef=
ore he could cancel them. For instance, in a chat on June 13, 2011, he expl=
ained to a colleague that, in order to execute a Genuine Order to sell, he =
had placed a buy order (a =E2=80=9Cbid=E2=80=9D) into the market. The colle=
ague asked, =E2=80=9Cwhy you try and bid? to spoof?=E2=80=9D Trader-1 answe=
red: =E2=80=9Cy[es] . . . i was doing lot of that last week & was sayin=
g myself, gonna get caught soon, should stop.=E2=80=9D In a chat two weeks =
later, on June 29, 2011, Trader1 mentioned to the same colleague that he ha=
d been =E2=80=9Ccauight spoofing few times,=E2=80=9D and in a chat on Augus=
t 15, 2012, he complained that he had =E2=80=9Cdropped little $ alraedy thi=
s am spoofing.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
These are Treasury markets. There is a lot of trading. Putting in one big o=
rder to buy might sometimes spook other traders into thinking there=E2=80=
=99s a ton of demand, so that they buy from you at a high price, which is w=
hat you secretly wanted. Or putting in one big order to buy might cause oth=
er traders to say =E2=80=9Coh great I had a ton of Treasuries to sell=E2=80=
=9D and selling them to you.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One other poin=
t about spoofing is that it is generally considered bad illegal manipulativ=
e spoofing to=C2=A0<em>show fake demand</em>, but it is just normal smart t=
rading to=C2=A0<em>hide your real demand</em>. So in my schematic descripti=
on above I said you could put in a real order to buy 100 things and a spoof=
 order to sell 1,000 things. But in practice in many markets what you=E2=80=
=99d do is put in a=C2=A0<em>hidden=C2=A0</em>order to buy 100 things, so o=
ther traders wouldn=E2=80=99t see your demand, and a=C2=A0<em>visible=C2=A0=
</em>spoof order to sell 1,000 things, so they would. That is what NatWest =
did:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">More specifically, on hund=
reds of occasions, the Subject NatWest Traders placed one or more orders fo=
r U.S. Treasuries that they intended to execute (=E2=80=9CGenuine Orders=E2=
=80=9D). Sometimes, but not always, the Genuine Orders were =E2=80=9Ciceber=
g=E2=80=9D orders, so that other market participants could see only a porti=
on of the order=E2=80=99s full size at any given time. An =E2=80=9Ciceberg=
=E2=80=9D order was a type of order that a trader could place on certain tr=
ading platforms and exchanges that did not display the order=E2=80=99s full=
 size to other market participants. Only a pre-set portion of an iceberg or=
der was visible at any given time. When the visible portion was filled, the=
 next pre-set portion of the order became visible, and so forth.=C2=A0</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">During the same trading sequences, the Subject =
NatWest Traders also placed one or more Spoof Orders on the opposite side o=
f the market from the Genuine Orders. The Spoof Orders were not iceberg ord=
ers, and so the full order size was visible to other market participants.=
=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The first part of that =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0hiding the real orders =E2=80=94=C2=A0is totally fine and no=
rmal and a built-in part of many trading venues. The second part =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0showing fake orders =E2=80=94=C2=A0is a crime. But when prosecutors s=
ay that the spoofing was bad because=C2=A0market participants believed =E2=
=80=9Cthat the=C2=A0visible order book accurately reflected market-based fo=
rces of supply and demand,=E2=80=9D there is a little asterisk there. Every=
one=C2=A0knew that lots of supply and demand wasn=E2=80=99t reflected on th=
e visible order book, and that was fine.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An=
yway at some point a NatWest trader got caught spoofing by another firm, wh=
ich complained to NatWest. And the complaint got to the trader=E2=80=99s su=
pervisor, who called him (on a recorded line) and said:</p><blockquote><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic complaint . . . is they=E2=80=99re tryin=

 you are doing is fair or not fair . . . . In a Darwinian sense I don=E2=80=
=99t have any issue with it . . . but the fact is they do provide liquidity=

ur activity . . . then I do have a problem with it. . . . [S]omeone who rea=

and then we go down the path of the nature of spoofing and whether you have=
 a job after it as well.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>=E2=80=9CThe nature of spoofing=E2=80=9D is a great imponderable mystery b=
